food_name: Red Bean Soup (Sweet, with Sago and Rock Sugar, Chinese Style)
region_of_origin: Guangdong / Fujian, China; widely adopted across all Chinese communities in Singapore
food_subgroup: Sweetened adzuki bean dessert soup with sago pearls and rock sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Chinese Sweet Red Bean Soup. Closest parent is a sweetened legume dessert soup. No exact FoodOn URI assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sweetened adzuki bean soup with sago pearls and rock sugar — Chinese red bean tong sui style
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0FGF is the closest FoodEx2 parent for sweetened legume-based dessert soups; no exact code exists for this specific preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Chinese sweet red bean soup as a specific food entity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Chinese sweet red bean soup served at a hawker stall or kopitiam is approximately 300 g, providing around 294 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Red Bean Soup (sweet, with sago, rock sugar) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Health Promotion Board Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Red Bean Soup (sweet, with sago, rock sugar). Core macros and secondary nutrients confirmed from this entry. Micronutrients copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (adzuki beans, cooked, boiled) where HPB data was not available, with confidence 0.9 for those fields.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Adzuki beans (cooked) and analogous sweetened legume soups
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: Adzuki beans (cooked, boiled) have a published GI of approximately 29–35 in the University of Sydney GI Database. The composite dish GI of ~40 accounts for the addition of rock sugar and sago pearls, which raise the effective GI modestly above the plain bean value. Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 67.5) / 100 = 27.0. This is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement of this exact preparation is available in the database.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (12 mg/100 g); beneficial potassium content (145 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Red bean soup is one of the lowest-sodium desserts available in the Singapore hawker setting, delivering only 36 mg sodium per 300 g bowl, while the potassium from adzuki beans supports favourable blood pressure regulation; it is suitable for patients with hypertension without modification.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low composite GI (~40) due to adzuki bean resistant starch and dietary fibre; moderate glycemic load per serving (GL 27)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite its sweet taste, red bean soup has a genuinely low GI (~40) driven by the resistant starch and high fibre content of adzuki beans, making it a significantly better dessert choice for blood glucose management than most other sweet desserts; however, the glycemic load per standard 300 g bowl is moderate (GL 27), so portion control remains important for patients with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat; dietary fibre 2.8 g/100 g; folate 32 mcg DFE/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Red bean soup has a highly favourable cardiovascular profile — zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero trans fat, and meaningful dietary fibre and folate from adzuki beans — making it a heart-healthy dessert choice suitable for patients managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile: folate 32 mcg DFE, iron 1.5 mg, magnesium 28 mg per 100 g; adzuki bean flavonoids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The anti-inflammatory micronutrient profile of red bean soup — including folate, non-haem iron, magnesium, and adzuki bean polyphenols — supports its suitability for patients with rheumatoid arthritis as an occasional dessert.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chinese Sweet Red Bean Soup (红豆汤 / 紅豆湯, Hóngdòutāng; also known as Hung Dou Sha / 紅豆沙) is one of the most widely consumed traditional Chinese desserts in Singapore, sold at hawker dessert stalls, kopitiam counters, and as takeaway cups throughout the island. It is prepared by slow-simmering dried adzuki (red) beans in water until they are extremely tender and partially dissolve into the broth, creating a naturally thick, slightly sandy texture known in Cantonese as 'sha.' The soup is sweetened with rock sugar and typically flavoured with pandan leaf; small sago pearls are added for a soft, translucent textural contrast. It may be served warm in cooler weather or chilled in summer. Nutritionally, red bean soup provides 98 kcal per 100 g, with 3.2 g protein, 0.2 g fat, 22.5 g carbohydrate, 14.5 g sugar, and 2.8 g dietary fibre. It is extremely low in sodium (12 mg/100 g) and contains zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and zero trans fat. A clinically important point: despite its sweet taste, red bean soup has a genuinely low composite GI of approximately 40, driven by the resistant starch and high fibre content of adzuki beans — one of the lowest-GI legumes available. The glycemic load per standard 300 g bowl is moderate (GL 27), so portion awareness is still warranted for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, but this dish is significantly better for blood glucose management than most other sweet desserts. It is vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, making it suitable across Singapore's diverse dietary communities. In traditional Chinese medicine, red bean soup is valued for its 'dampness-removing' and blood-nourishing properties.
common_user_queries: is red bean soup good for diabetes
red bean soup GI glycemic index
red bean soup vs green bean soup

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker foods. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using published adzuki bean GI values, with the composite dish GI adjusted for added sugar and sago. Micronutrients copper, manganese, and vitamin K supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with confidence 0.9. All mandatory multilingual names present. Ontology anchored to FoodEx2 closest parent.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this traditional dessert soup preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central adzuki bean entries as of April 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this specific preparation is not reported in the HPB database. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB and USDA sources). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin D-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per HPB source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — vegan preparation with no animal-derived ingredients). saturated_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source; adzuki beans and sago contain negligible saturated fat). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no hydrogenated fats used). Portions: the primary default serving is 1 bowl = 300 g per HPB Singapore Foodo; a secondary 250 g serving size is also noted. The duplicate 250 g portion entry in the input has been deduplicated. GI note: no direct GI measurement of this exact preparation (with sago and rock sugar) is available in the University of Sydney GI Database; the composite GI of ~40 is an analogous estimate based on published adzuki bean GI (29–35) adjusted upward for added sugar and sago contribution. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (40 × 67.5) / 100 = 27.0.

improvement_actions_taken: Restructured record to conform to RESULT_template.json field names and structure.
Added mandatory Hindi local name (लाल बीन सूप (चीनी शैली)) absent from input.
Added Tamil alias (லால் பீன் சூப்) and Hindi alias to aliases and aliases_by_language.
Converted flat aliases array from object-style to string-style per template.
Ensured all aliases_by_language strings appear verbatim in flat aliases array (q8 self-check).
Added health_context with all three required conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) using correct template structure and human_condition_name.
Documented -1 sentinel values (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, water_g) in notes with reason and source consulted.
Documented confirmed-zero values (vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg, cholesterol_mg, saturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g) in notes.
Deduplicated duplicate 250 g portion entry from input.
Added foodex2_gap_note and snomed_ct_gap_note to ontology section.
Set gi_source as structured object per template.

Verified energy check: 98 kcal ≈ (3.2 × 4) + (0.2 × 9) + (22.5 × 4) = 12.8 + 1.8 + 90.0 = 104.6 kcal — slight discrepancy consistent with dietary fibre partial exclusion from energy calculation in Asian food databases; accepted as per HPB source.
